coredata.dc:title: Selective FRET-based sensing of 4-nitrophenol and cell imaging capitalizing on the fluorescent properties of carbon nanodots from apple seeds
coredata.dc:creator: Chatzimarkou, Anna
Chatzimitakos, Theodoros G.
Kasouni, Athanasia
Sygellou, Lamprini
Avgeropoulos, Apostolos
Stalikas, Constantine D.
coredata.dc:description: The selective detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is deemed important from an environmental and biochemical point of view. Herein, the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) from apple seeds, as a carbon and nitrogen source is described, using a single-step pyrolysis method. The synthesized nitrogen-doped CNDs emit intense blue fluorescence, which can effectively be quenched by 4-NP, via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The ‘green’ CNDs are properly characterized and examined for their fluorescent and stability properties. The CNDs are proved to be a successful sensing nanoprobe for the development of an analytical method towards the sensitive and selective detection of 4-NP in environmental water samples and human urine. The high availability and low value of the precursor along with the low cost of synthesis and analysis and the selective sensing are the major advantages of the proposed method. The wide linear range (0.05–53.0μM), the low limit of detection (13nM) and the acceptable sample recoveries (from 101 to 112%) render the proposed sensing method an improved alternative to previously proposed methods. Finally, the CNDs are explored for cell imaging in three cell lines, with excellent biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, suggesting their great potential in bioimaging and other biosensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: 4-Nitrophenol sensing
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
Apple seeds
FRET mechanism
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CNDs from apple seeds
Behavior of CNDs under different conditions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Deconvoluted XPS peaks of C1s (a), O1s (b) and N1s (c) of CNDs.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of CNDs. Inset: Images of aqueous solution of CNDs under UV (A) and visible (B) light.
(A) Excitation dependent emission spectra and (B) excitation spectrum at emission of 380nm of the CNDs.
The decrease of PL intensity of CNDs caused by consecutive additions of 4-NP from 2.8μΜ to 81.0μΜ.
PL quenching of CNDs caused by phenols and metal ions.
Calibration curve for the detection of 4-NP; Inset: linear range of the calibration curve.
Effect of temperature on the quenching of the CNDs PL.
Normalized excitation and emission spectra of CNDs and absorption spectrum of 4-NP.
Fluorescence microscopy images of (Α) untreated and apple seed CNDs-treated (B) MDA-MB-231, (C) A549, (D) HEK-293 under UV (1) and visible (2) light. Magnification 40×.
Analyses of real samples for 4-NP and recoveries, at three spiking concentration levels.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Values in parentheses represent the concentrations determined by an HPLC method using Hypersil BDS C18 (200mm×4.0mm, 5μm particle size) column and diode array detector.

head.ce:title: Selective FRET-based sensing of 4-nitrophenol and cell imaging capitalizing on the fluorescent properties of carbon nanodots from apple seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry, Department of Chemistry, University of Ioannina, 45110 Ioannina, Greece

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Biophysical Chemistry, Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biological Applications and Technologies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT), Stadiou Str., P.O. Box 1414, GR – 26504, Rio-Patras, Greece

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Foundation for Research and Technology Hellas/Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (FORTH/ICE-HT)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science Engineering, University of Ioannina, 45110, Ioannina, Greece

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The selective detection of 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is deemed important from an environmental and biochemical point of view. Herein, the synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) from apple seeds, as a carbon and nitrogen source is described, using a single-step pyrolysis method. The synthesized nitrogen-doped CNDs emit intense blue fluorescence, which can effectively be quenched by 4-NP, via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. The ‘green’ CNDs are properly characterized and examined for their fluorescent and stability properties. The CNDs are proved to be a successful sensing nanoprobe for the development of an analytical method towards the sensitive and selective detection of 4-NP in environmental water samples and human urine. The high availability and low value of the precursor along with the low cost of synthesis and analysis and the selective sensing are the major advantages of the proposed method. The wide linear range (0.05–53.0μM), the low limit of detection (13nM) and the acceptable sample recoveries (from 101 to 112%) render the proposed sensing method an improved alternative to previously proposed methods. Finally, the CNDs are explored for cell imaging in three cell lines, with excellent biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity, suggesting their great potential in bioimaging and other biosensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenols are well known deleterious compounds, since their high water solubility and stability render them difficult to remove from the environment [1]. The 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) is a resonance-stabilized nitrophenol and as a result it is highly stable; however, it is toxic and can cause nausea, eye irritation, cyanosis or even be fatal for organisms [2]. Thus, U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (U.S.EPA) has listed it as a priority environmental pollutant, while its concentration in drinking water must be limited to concentrations under 60ngml−1 [3,4]. Because of its widespread applicability in the manufacturing process of drugs, dyes, fungicides and insecticides, as well as of the occurrence as degradation product of ethyl and methyl parathion, its release in the environment is considerable [4–6]. Consequently, monitoring the levels of 4-NP in the environment is deemed important for protection and health reasons. On the other hand, 4-NP can serve as a biological marker in urine, as it helps to assess the magnitude of exposure to various pesticides [7] or is produced by particular enzymes, in the presence of the corresponding substrates [8].

It is more than obvious that the development of rapid, sensitive and precise methods is always welcome due to the ongoing need to upgrade or replace already existing ones or to provide reasonable alternatives. Up to now, several analytical methods have been reported for the detection of 4-NP, based on spectrophotometry [9], electroanalytical techniques [10], chromatographic techniques [11] and chemiluminescence [12].

Lately, carbon nanodots (CNDs), a bright, up-and-coming class of carbon-based nanomaterials have triggered a great deal of studies regarding their synthesis and applications. More specifically, CNDs are quasi-spherical nanoparticles, consisting mainly of sp 2/sp 3 carbon, oxygen and/or other doping elements [12,13]. Primarily, their photoluminescence (PL) properties and secondarily their excellent solubility, the chemical inertness and low toxicity, distinguish them from other carbon-based nanomaterials and make feasible their application in new fields, such as sensing, drug delivery and gene transfer, in vitro and in vivo imaging etc. [14,15]. In this context, lately, CNDs were used to develop analytical procedures based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism between CNDs and the target compounds, such as ammonia [16,17], melamine [18] etc. Regarding the synthesis of CNDs, different methods have been reported and various carbon sources (Citrus peels, goose feathers, saffron, waste polyolefins etc.) have been exploited, since their PL behavior depends on the precursor material(s) [14,19–23]. Zhou et al. developed graphene-based carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for the detection of 4-NP in environmental matrices [24], using a molecularly-imprinted polymer hybridized with the dots, while Ahmed et al. put forward a method for the environmental determination of 4-NP using CNDs prepared by thermal carbonization of ethyleneglycol bis-(2-aminoethyl ether)-N,N,N’,N’-tetraacetic acid [4].

However, the latest trend in the synthesis of CNDs, revolves around the use of natural and residual resources as starting materials, in order to lessen the environmental impact of the synthesis [25]. In addition, a more viable and preferable alternative option is the re-use of waste materials or natural resources, with multiple inherent advantages, such as abundance of carbon sources from low-value precursor materials, varieties of heteroatom doping (such as N, S, P), saving of chemical precursors for other uses, addressing of environmental issues caused by waste disposal etc. [23,26,27]. In this context, we have recently developed two analytical methods for the determination of iron and tartrazine from Citrus peels CNDs [19].

Apples are widely consumed worldwide and their world production during the year 2015 was higher than 70 million metric tons [28]. The wastes from the processes for apple-based products are usually turned into apple pomace, but the presence of the apple seeds during the production of the pomace and natural toxins like amygdalin that can be contained, rise concerns over its safety [29]. Apple seeds consist mainly of fat, protein, amino acids [30] and due to the presence of amygdalin, they can serve as an excellent source of carbon and nitrogen in order to produce nitrogen-doped CNDs.

In this study, the production of CNDs from apple seeds via a single-step, green synthesis is described. The production of the ‘green’ CNDs leads to the development of a FRET-based, sensitive and straightforward sensing approach, seeking to address the need for an improved analytical method or alternative to those previously reported for 4-NP. Furthermore, the excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity of apple seed-derived CNDs against various cell lines along with their optical merits reinforce their applicability potential as cell imaging agent.
All information about chemicals and materials is provided in the Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CNDs from apple seeds
ce:section.ce:para: Seeds from fresh apples (Malus domestica 'Fuji') were rinsed with DDW and left to air-dry. Then, the seeds were placed in a crucible and pyrolized in a furnace, at 300°C, for 1h. The black residue was crashed with a mortar, in order to obtain a homogeneous black powder, which was dispersed in 50mL DDW through ultrasonication, for 10min. To remove large particles, the dispersion was centrifuged at 8000rpm, for 5min. The supernatant was collected and dialyzed through a Visking dialysis tubing membrane (MWCO: 12.000–14.000Da) against water, for 24h. The outer solution, free from bulky carbonaceous particles, was collected and freeze-dried, resulting in a yellow-brown solid powder. The dried CNDs product was stored in dark, until use.
Details about the instrumentation for sensing, characterization and preparation of samples are given in the Supplementary material.
The QYs of the as-synthesized CNDs were determined by a comparative method. Details are given in Supplementary material.

The detection of 4-NP was carried out as follows: 1mL of sample was added to 2mL of NH3/NH4Cl buffer solution (pH 9.0, 0.15M), containing 5μg of the CNDs. After vortexing the mixture for 10s, the PL intensity was recorded at λex/λem =330/380nm and the quantification of 4-NP content in the sample was carried out using a calibration curve, which was built by plotting the fluorescence quenching – after monitoring the original (F 0) and quenched (F) fluorescence – against quencher concentrations. Environmental water samples were analyzed directly, without any pretreatment, except for a filtration step to remove particulate matter, whereas human urine were diluted 1:1000 (v/v) prior analysis to avoid background fluorescence. The selectivity was evaluated by measuring the fluorescence quenching percentages provoked by other metal ions or potential matrix components.
Information regarding cell growth and cell viability assay are described in the Supplementary material.
Experimental conditions for cellular imaging are provided in the Supplementary material.

Τhe FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1 of Supplementary material) exhibits a broad adsorption peak at 3279cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibration of OH and NH groups, whereas the broad, low-intensity peak around 2930cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of sp 3 CH [31,32]. The stretching and bending vibrations of CC and NH2 can be seen at 1645cm−1, while the CO stretching and COH bending of carboxylic acid are associated with the peaks at 1240 and 1440cm−1, respectively. Finally, the peaks at 1550 and 1040cm−1 are attributed to NH and CN stretching, respectively. The occurrence of such functional groups can enhance the stability and hydrophilicity of the resulting CNDs in aqueous systems [33].

The XRD pattern of the synthesized CNDs (Fig. S2 of Supplementary material) shows a wide diffraction peak located around 22°, which corresponds to an interlayer spacing of about 0.404nm and indicates an amorphous carbon structure [34]. TEM images of the synthesized CNDs (Fig. S3 of Supplementary material) reveal that the size of the CNDs is typically smaller than 3nm, while their size distribution is narrow.

An XPS study was performed for the surface characterization of nitrogen-doped CNDs. Fig. 1 shows the detailed core level peaks of the combined C1s-K2p window, of the O1s and N1s. The C1s peak (Fig. 1a) is analyzed into five components, C1-C5, assigned to CC sp 2 and defective sp 3 bonds, at binding energies of 284.7eV and 285.6eV, respectively, to carbon−oxygen hydroxyls at 286.4eV, to carbonyls at 288.3eV and to a small contribution of carboxyl at 291.5eV. In the same window, the K2p peak at binding energy of K2p3/2 at 292.8eV is assigned to KO bond [35]. The deconvoluted O1s peak (Fig. 1b) consists of three components assigned to carbon−oxygen double bonds at 531.1eV, to single bonds at 532.6eV and to a low-intensity component at 534eV assigned to adsorbed H2O [36]. A minor contribution of sample holder is present in both C1s and O1s peaks. The fractions of both C−O and CO (COOH) components, as derived from C1s peak fitting and the defective sp 3 bonds, are shown in Table S1 of Supplementary material. The same trend is confirmed by the components CO and CO of O1s peak deconvolution. The deconvoluted N1s peak (Fig. 1c) afford two components, at binding energies of 399.2eV and 400.1eV, which are assigned to pyridinic N surface groups (NC(H)) and amine/amide or pyrolic moieties (NC(H)), respectively [37]. The fractions of nitrogen-containing groups and the relative atomic composition of basic elements are showed in Table S1.

For the purpose of enhancing the yield of the single-step synthesis, the combining effect of pyrolysis temperature and time was optimized. The CNDs were tested and the criteria used to assess the most efficient synthetic route was the PL intensity of the CNDs and the % PL quenching caused by 4-NP. The resulting products exhibited the same patterns of excitation and emission spectra, except for CNDs produced after 3h of carbonization, at 250°C (optimum λex/λem =380/490). Nonetheless, the PL of all synthesized CNDs was equally quenched in the presence of 4-NP. As a result, the PL intensity of the product was used as the sole criterion for the pyrolysis optimization. Pyrolysis at 300°C for 1h, led to the product with the highest PL intensity (Fig. S4 of Supplementary material) and therefore, this combination was selected as the optimum.

The optical features of the as-synthesized CNDs were evaluated at room temperature by recording UV–vis and PL emission spectra. The UV–vis spectrum depicted in Fig. 2 , illustrates two broad absorption peaks at around 270nm and 340nm. The former can be assigned to the π-π* transition of aromatic conjugated CC bond [38] and the latter to the n-π* transition in the oxygen of CO bond [39] or to the transitions of CC and CN bonds due to the formation of excited defect surface states caused by N [40]. Aqueous solutions of the CNDs exhibit a faint yellow color, under daylight and an intense blue PL, under UV light (Fig. 2, inset).

The PL emission spectra of CNDs at different excitation wavelengths, from 290nm to 370nm, are illustrated in Fig. 3 A. The emission spectra showed two maxima at around 380nm and 415nm, when the CNDs were excited from 290 to 340nm. The dual emission spectra can be attributed to the fact that the synthesis of CNDs via pyrolysis produces CO2, which escapes from the system leading to the formation of carbogenic cores. It has been reported that cores co-exist with the molecular fluorophores and as a result, the fluorescent behavior of CNDs is associated with a mixture of sp 2 and sp 3 carbons, which are responsible for the dual-emission [41]. For the as-synthesized CNDs, the longer the excitation wavelength the more enhanced the PL intensity, up to 330nm. As shown in Fig. 3B, the strongest PL intensity of emission band is observed when the CNDs are excited at 330nm. At this excitation wavelength, a strong PL emission band is centered at 380nm. Also, it can be seen that the CNDs display excitation wavelength-dependent emission properties, that is, the maximum fluorescence emission shifts from 390nm and 440nm as the excitation wavelength varies from 350 to 370nm. This apparent PL band shift is rather common to CNDs and may be attributed to the alternation of single-double bond segments in carbon clusters with sp 2 hybridization [42]. Finally, the QY of the resulting CNDs was found to be 20%, which is higher than those derived from other green syntheses CNDs [27,43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Behavior of CNDs under different conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Major factors which can affect the applicability of nitrogen-doped CNDs are the pH, the ionic strength and the presence of humic acids, for testing environmental matrixes. Therefore, a stability study was conducted to evaluate their effect on the PL intensity of the prepared CNDs. The increase of pH from 1.0 to 6.0 is accompanied by a progressive rise of PL of the CNDs (Fig. S5 of Supplementary material). An apparent PL plateau is noticed between pH 6.0 and 9.0 followed by a significant decrease at pH >10, which implies a strong dependence of PL intensity on the pH. The reason for this behavior may be the dissociation of ionisable groups of CNDs – like carboxyl groups – in basic solution, which implies that overly basic environment may induce changes of functional groups and then the electronic transition of some defects would be disrupted or even prohibited [45].

As regards the PL of aqueous CNDs solutions with different ionic strength, they seem to be stable at any concentration of NaCl or Na2SO4 up to 1.0mol/L. On the contrary, results showed that humic acids can reduce the emitted PL of CNDs. It is true that the broad-band intensive absorption of humic acids (Fig. S6 of Supplementary material) is a serious reason for concern about the inner filter effect in fluorescent CQD systems [46]. This issue is rather overlooked in the literature. In our case, as the concentration of humic acids increases (from 0 to 10mgL−1), a reduction of nearly 10% in the PL intensity is observed.

Finally, the PL measurement of a CNDs solution, stored at room temperature for one week, revealed that the solution scarcely exhibited any variation in the emitted PL (<0.1% compared to the measurement on the first day). Furthermore, continuous irradiation of CNDs solution for 3h with a 150W Xe lamp had no effect on the PL intensity, bespeaking satisfactory photostability. The stability of response of the as-prepared CNDs, under these conditions, holds out the promising prospect of applicability of the material.

The intrinsic PL properties of the studied CNDs motivated a series of experiments to evaluate them as a sensing nanoprobe. Preliminary data proved that the apple seed-derived CNDs can act as an effective PL probe for the detection of 4-NP. Τhe addition of 52.5μM 4-NP in an aqueous solution of CNDs caused a considerable quenching of the emitted PL (76%). From Fig. 4 , it can be seen that aside from the reduction of the maximum PL in the presence of different concentrations of 4-NP, a blue shift of the maximum emission up to 15nm is also recorded, probably due to the more hydrophobic environment around the fluorophores of the CNDs, when 4-NP is present [47].

Since 4-NP is an ionizable analyte and the PL of CNDs is pH-dependent, experiments were conducted so as to identify the optimum pH value that favors the quenching caused by 4-NP. Fig. S7 of Supplementary material shows that a PL quenching of 75% is provoked in the pH the range of 1.0–5.0, while a 15% augmentation of the quenching is evidenced by further increasing the pH up to 9. At higher pH, no increase of the quenching is seen. As the effect of pH on the performance of the CNDs – 4-NP sensing system is important, all experiments were conducted using a NH3/NH4Cl buffer solution (pH 9.0) in order to minimize quenching variations, due to pH alterations.

Further experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of ionic strength and the presence of humic acids in the fall of PL caused by 4-NP. The quenching remained almost constant in aqueous solutions of NaCl and Na2SO4 with varying concentrations, in the range of 0–1.0mol/L, while the same behavior was observed in the presence of humic acids, at concentrations up to 10mgL−1. Although humic acids reduce the maximum PL intensity of the CNDs, as mentioned above, they do not interfere with the quenching of PL at a considerable degree, in the presence of 4-NP, since a negligible positive error (<3.0%) arises from their presence in a water sample.

As the above discussion attests, the synthesized CNDs can serve as a potential sensing system for the detection of 4-NP. Before establishing the analytical method, a selectivity study was undertaken to validate the reliability and feasibility of the developed chemosensor towards 4-NP. Metal ions, other phenols and amino acids (50μM) were examined as potential interfering substances for their ability to quench the PL of the CNDs. From the results depicted in Fig. 5 (and Fig. S8 of Supplementary material), it is obvious that the presence of 4-NP, causes, by far, the strongest quenching of PL. The presence of other substituted and non-substituted phenols, metal ions and amino acids in aqueous solutions of CQDs, induce insignificant drop in the PL intensity, except for 2-NP and Hg2+. From the above results, along with the fact that 4-NP is the most frequently occurring nitrophenol in environmental matrices [4]], it can be inferred that the potential interferences do not affect the feasibility, reliability and capacity for 4-NP sensing.

Fig. 6 portrays the ratio F0 /F versus the concentration of 4-NP, ranging from 0.01 to 81.6μM. The PL quenching showed a linear relation with the 4-NP concentration in the range of 0.05–53.0μM, with a coefficient of determination (R 2) of 0.9939. The detection limit calculated as signal-to-noise ratio ≥3 was found to be 13nM, which is lower than those of previously reported methods using CNDs (Table S2 of Supplementary material). The applicability of the as-synthesized CNDs for sensing 4-NP in real water samples such as tap, river, lake and fountain water, as well as human urine was evaluated by recovery experiments and the method was compared to an HPLC one, at low spiking levels. As it can be deduced from Table 1 , the recoveries at a low concentration of 4-NP (6.0μM) ranged from 102 to 112% and those at medium and higher concentrations (20.2 and 41.3μΜ) were equally satisfactory, ranging between 101–106% and 101–102%, respectively. The detection of 4-NP in these matrices is highly sensitive and is not affected to a measurable degree, by potential interfering compounds. Because of the above merits, under certain substrate conditions, the applicability of the method can conceivably be expanded to the FRET-based sensing of particular enzymes, like a-glucosidase and alkaline phosphatase, where 4-NP is the enzyme catalytic product [8].

The PL quenching exhibits a strong dependence on the concentration of the quencher (Fig. 6), which is described by the Stern-Volmer equation [48]:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
S V
[ Q ]
where F0 and F are the PL intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively; [Q] is the concentration of the quencher (i.e. 4-NP) and Ksv is the Stern-Volmer constant. The Stern-Volmer plots were prepared at three different temperatures (i.e. 25, 35 and 45°C) and at concentrations of 4-NP below 52.8μΜ (Fig. 7 ). As it can be seen, a temperature rise leads to an apparent reduction in the PL intensity, which is more pronounced at the highest temperature studied. This inverse temperature-dependence behavior is indicative of static quenching mechanism. By increasing temperature, a dissociation of weakly bound complexes occurs and hence lower percentage of quenching is achieved, indicating the static nature of the quenching.

Fig. 8 reveals an overlap between the absorption spectrum of 4-NP and emission spectrum of CNDs, which implies an energy transfer from the excited state of the donor (i.e. CNDs) to the acceptor (i.e. 4-NP) through non-radiative dipole–dipole coupling, the so-called Förster resonance energy transfer mechanism. This apparent overlap can be described by the FRET equation for spectral overlap, which expresses the degree of spectral overlap between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption (Eq. (2)):
(2)
J
( λ )
=
∫
0
∞
F D
( λ )
ε A
( λ )
λ
4
d λ
∫
0
∞
F D
( λ )
d λ
( i n
M
− 1
c
m
− 1
n
m
4
)
where ε A(λ) is the acceptor extinction coefficient of the acceptor at λ wavelength (M−1 cm−1), F D(λ) is the PL intensity of the donor in the wavelength λ +Δλ [48].

To confirm that the FRET mechanism is definitely applicable in the studied system, factors such as the Förster distance (R 0) and the distance between the acceptor and the donor (r) were calculated and evaluated, according to Eqs. (3) and (4), respectively.
(3)
R 0 6
= 8.79 ×
10
− 5
k 2
n
− 4
Q D
J
( i n
Å 6
)
(4)
E = 1 −
F
F 0
=
R 0 6
R 0 6
+
r 6
where k2 is the orientation factor (in most cases is assumed to be 2/3); Q D is the quantum yield of the donor in the absence of the acceptor and n is the refractive index. After calculating the above, the J, R 0, r and E were found to be 3.90×10−14 M−1 cm−1 nm4, 6.78×10−4 Å, 2.66nm and 0.72, respectively. As r is lower than 8nm and lies within the range of 0.5R 0 < r< 2R 0, the FRET mechanism can also describe the quenching of the as-studied system [48]. The 4-NP is a weak acid (pK a =7.15) which is fully deprotonated at pH 9.0. The potential formation of a Meisenheimer complex [4,49] is speculated to bring the donor and acceptor close to each other and as a direct consequence, to assist the transfer of energy between CNDs and 4-NP. Therefore, an effective FRET-based fluorescence sensing platform is established, based on the unique emission of CNDs and the induced effective fluorescence quenching by 4-NP.

The CNDs synthesized from natural organics hold great promise for sensing agents. In this study, highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped CNDs were synthesized by a single-step pyrolysis method using apple seeds as raw material. The dots emitted strong blue fluorescence with high stability. Using the CNDs as fluorescence nanoprobe, an effective and facile FRET-based sensing approach was developed for the determination of 4-NP. The method is highly reproducible, selective and sensitive and the starting material required for the CNDs have the advantage of being cheap, easily available and environmentally friendly. The CNDs from apple seeds can be used for in-vitro imaging and have potential for other biosensing applications, such as the FRET-based sensing of particular enzymes, whose catalytic product is 4-NP.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Many thanks are due to the XRD center of University of Ioannina for obtaining the XRD spectra.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.11.182.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Chemicals and materials. Instrumentation and characterization. Quantum yield. Cell viability assay. Cellular imaging. FT-IR spectrum, XRD pattern, TEM image of apple seed CNDs. PL intensity as a function of pyrolysis temperature and time. Effect of pH on the PL intensity of CNDs and quenching caused by 4-NP. Cytotoxicity of CNDs on different cell lines. Percent of sp 2, sp 3, C−O and CO bonds, pyridine-like (NC(H)) and amine/amide or pyrolic (NC(H)) and atomic content of carbon, oxygen, potassium and nitrogen. Comparison of the CNDs with others reported.

sb:author.ce:surname: Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry (ATSDR)

sb:publisher.sb:name: Public Health Service, U.S. Department of Health and Human Services

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comparative study of toxicity of 4-nitrophenol and 2,4-dinitrophenol in newborn and young rats
Highly fluorescent carbon dots as nanoprobes for sensitive and selective determination of 4-nitrophenol in surface waters
One-pot green synthesis of Prussian blue nanocubes decorated reduced graphene oxide using mushroom extract for efficient 4-nitrophenol reduction
Extraction of trace nitrophenols in environmental water samples using boronate affinity sorbent
Application of para-nitrophenol (pNP) enzyme assays in degraded tropical soils
Quantification of phenolic metabolites of environmental chemicals in human urine using gas chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry and isotope dilution quantification
Electrochemical sensing and remediation of 4-nitrophenol using bio-synthesized copper oxide nanoparticles
Analysis of nitrophenols in cloud water with a miniaturized light-phase rotary perforator and HPLC-MS
Highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol and Fe3+ ion based on a luminescent layered terbium (III) coordination polymer
Highly luminescent N, S- Co-doped carbon dots and their direct use as mercury(II) sensor
Review on carbon dots and their applications
Nitrogen doped carbon dots derived from Sargassum fluitans as fluorophore for DNA detection
FRET based ammonia sensor using carbon dots
Carbon dots and fluorescein: the ideal FRET pair for the fabrication of a precise and fully reversible ammonia sensor
A carbon dot based biosensor for melamine detection by fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Two of a kind but different: luminescent carbon quantum dots from Citrus peels for iron and tartrazine sensing and cell imaging
A facile microwave-hydrothermal approach towards highly photoluminescent carbon dots from goose feathers
Determination of sunset yellow in soft drinks based on fluorescence quenching of carbon dots
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.
A novel one-step and green synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon dots from saffron for cell imaging and sensing of prilocaine
Synthesis of green fluorescent carbon quantum dots using waste polyolefins residue for Cu2+ ion sensing and live cell imaging
A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
Waste frying oil as a precursor for one-step synthesis of sulfur-doped carbon dots with pH-sensitive photoluminescence
Green synthesis of carbon dots from rose-heart radish and application for Fe3+ detection and cell imaging
Multivalorization of apple pomace towards materials and chemicals. Waste to wealth
Apple pomace and products derived from apple pomace: uses, composition and analysis
Proximate composition of the apple seed and characterization of its oil
Infrared and Raman Spectroscopy: Methods and Applications
A facile synthesis of water-soluble carbon dots as a label-free fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of picric acid
Fe3O4–Graphene nanocomposites with improved lithium storage and magnetism properties
Surface characterization of Co,K/La2O3 catalysts used for the catalytic combustion of diesel soot
Work function tuning of reduced graphene oxide thin films
Nitrogen-doped carbon derived from ZIF-8 as a high-performance metal-free catalyst for acetylene hydrochlorination
One pot synthesis of highly fluorescent N doped C-dots and used as fluorescent probe detection for Hg2+ and Ag+ in aqueous solution
Multicolor nitrogen-doped carbon dots for live cell imaging
Microwave-assisted one-pot synthesis of highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots for cellular imaging and multi-ion probing
Formation mechanism of carbogenic nanoparticles with dual photoluminescence emission
Chemically derived graphene oxide: towards large-area thin-film electronics and optoelectronics
Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from lemon peel waste: applications in sensing and photocatalysis
Green synthesis of carbon nanodots from cotton for multicolor imaging, patterning, and sensing
Easy synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon quantum dots from gelatin and their luminescent properties and applications
Fluorescence quenching method for determining equilibrium constants for polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons binding to dissolved humic materials
Characterizing the interaction between tartrazine and two serum albumins by a hybrid spectroscopic approach
Meisenheimer complex from picric acid and diisopropylcarbodiimide
Carbon Dots: synthesis, bioimaging, and biosafety assessment

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Anna Chatzimarkou received her Bachelor degree from the Department of Chemistry of University of Ioannina, Greece, in 2015. Currently, she is MSc candidate in the same Department. Her research interests focus on the interactions of carbon-based nanomaterials with environmental pollutants.

Theodoros Chatzimitakos received his MSc from the Department of University of Ioannina, Greece, in 2015. He is currently pursuing a PhD in Analytical Chemistry, at the Chemistry Department of University of Ioannina. His main scientific interest is the development of (bio)analytical methods based on nanomaterials.

Athanasia Kasouni received her Bachelor from the Department of Biological Applications and Technologies, of University of Ioannina, Greece, in 2014. Currently, she is pursuing a PhD in the same Department. Her scientific interests include the cytotoxicity assessment of carbon-based nanomaterials and their utilization in biological applications.

Dr. Sygellou Lamprini received her Bachelor degree from the Department of Physics of University of Ioannina, in 1999. Then, she joined the Department of Chemical Engineering of University of Patras, Greece, where she received her MSc degree and PhD, in 2005. Now, she is member of the Composites and Nanomaterials Laboratory the of Institute of Chemical Engineering Sciences (ICE-HT) and her interests focus on the characterization of materials with surface science techniques and the preparation and characterization of graphene-like substrates for the study of their electronic, optical and structural properties.

Prof. Apostolos Avgeropoulos received his Bachelor degree and PhD from the Department of Chemistry of University of Athens, in 1992 and 1997, respectively. In 1997 he joined the Department of Materials Science & Engineering of Massachusetts Institute of Technology, at Boston, USA, as postdoctoral associate. In 2003, he became assistant professor in the Department of Material Science and Engineering of University of Ioannina. His current research interests focus on the synthesis and characterization of polymeric materials for nanotechnology, biology and pharmacology.

Prof. Constantine Stalikas received his Bachelor degree and PhD from the Department of Chemistry of University of Ioannina, in 1988 and 1994, respectively. He joined the Department of Chemistry of University of Cordoba, Spain, in 1997 and the Institute of Hydrochemistry of Technical University of Munich, Germany, in 2000. In the same year, he became a lecturer in the Department of Chemistry of University of Ioannina. His current research interests focus on the development of analytical methods using carbon quantum dots, as sensing probes, from natural and residual resources.
